reasoning: The distribution of conflict fatalities in Indonesia (largely driven by the insurgency in Papua) structurally tends to be right-skewed, meaning that the median is routinely lower than the mean. Because one or two high-fatality incidents can significantly raise the 360-day average, a random 30-day period has a less than 50% probability of exceeding this trailing mean. The theoretical probability for a negative-binomial-like variable to exceed its mean is generally around 30-40%. Additionally, the resolution period falls roughly in the rainy season, which historically decreases rebel mobility and lowers the rate of violent confrontations in the highlands of Papua. Therefore, I estimate the probability of exceeding the 360-day average at 35%.
